statement: Multiple Icelandic governments have promised or discussed holding a referendum on EU accession without delivering one, creating a persistent source of political frustration. Key instances: (1) The 2009–2013 coalition of Samfylkingin and VG submitted Iceland's EU application in July 2009 with the stated intention of holding a referendum on any accession treaty, but the application was suspended before reaching that stage. (2) The 2013–2016 coalition (Progressive Party and Independence Party) pledged in its coalition agreement to withdraw the EU application, which was formally done in March 2015 — Foreign Minister Gunnar Bragi Sveinsson stated that no referendum was needed because the application was being withdrawn, not advanced. (3) The 2021–2024 coalition did not include an EU referendum in its programme. The 2024 coalition (Samfylkingin, Viðreisn, Flokkur fólksins) is the first government to explicitly commit to holding a referendum on resuming accession negotiations, scheduling it for 29 August 2026.
source_name: Althingi records; coalition agreements 2009, 2013, 2017, 2021, 2024; Icelandic media archives
caveats: Whether previous governments 'promised' a referendum depends on interpretation. The 2009 coalition committed to a referendum on any final accession treaty (which was never reached), not on whether to start negotiations. The characterisation that governments 'didn't keep their promise' conflates different types of commitments. Only the 2024 coalition has made an explicit commitment to a referendum on resuming negotiations per se.

related_entries.statement: The current Icelandic government is a coalition of Samfylkingin (Social Democrats, 13 seats), Viðreisn (Reform Party, 11 seats), and Flokkur fólksins (People's Party, 8 seats), holding 32 of 63 Althingi seats. The Prime Minister is Kristrún Frostadóttir (Samfylkingin). This is the first government coalition where both explicitly pro-EU parties (Samfylkingin and Viðreisn) serve together, which has been cited as a factor in advancing the EU referendum.

Iceland's previous EU accession negotiations were conducted from 2010 to 2013. Össur Skarphéðinsson (Samfylkingin) served as Foreign Minister from 2009 to 2013 and led the accession process. The application was submitted on 16 July 2009 under PM Jóhanna Sigurðardóttir's government (Samfylkingin–VG coalition). Negotiations progressed through 11 of 33 chapters before the incoming Sjálfstæðisflokkur–Framsóknarflokkur government under PM Sigmundur Davíð Gunnlaugsson effectively froze the process in 2013. Iceland formally withdrew its application on 12 March 2015 under Foreign Minister Gunnar Bragi Sveinsson (Framsóknarflokkur).

The Icelandic government announced in early 2026 that a national referendum on resuming EU accession negotiations would be held on 29 August 2026. The referendum is based on a parliamentary resolution (þingsályktunartillaga) submitted by the Foreign Minister. The three coalition parties — Samfylkingin (Social Democrats), Viðreisn (Reform Party), and Flokkur fólksins (People's Party) — support holding the referendum. The referendum question concerns whether Iceland should resume the accession negotiations that were suspended in 2013, not whether Iceland should join the EU outright. This is an advisory referendum (ráðgefandi þjóðaratkvæðagreiðsla), as Icelandic constitutional law does not provide for binding referenda on policy questions (the only binding referendum mechanism is under Article 26 for presidential vetoes of legislation).

citations.report_title: 25. þáttur Silfursins: Stefnt að kosningu um ESB í sumar

claims.claim_text: Stjórnarandstöðuflokkarnir lofuðu að fara með ESB-málið áfram í þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslu 2013 en gerðu það ekki og fóru ekki heldur með það fyrir utanríkismálanefnd.

Aðildarviðræður Jóhönnu Sigurðardóttur og Steingríms J. Sigfússonar steytti á skeri; þau gátu ekki klárað málið og gátu ekki farið með það í þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslu vegna þess að þau sáu að þau fengju ekki þær undanþágur sem ríkisstjórnarflokkarnir nú halda að þeir fái.
Það er fordæmalaust að ríki ætli að sækja um ESB-aðild þar sem ekki er meirihluti fyrir því á þingi og ekki einu sinni í ríkisstjórn.

claims.claim_text: Fyrri ríkisstjórnir lofuðu þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslu um ESB-aðild en efndu ekki loforðið.

claims.claim_text: Núverandi stjórnvöld halda því fram að aðildarumsóknin frá 2010 hafi aldrei verið formlega dregin til baka.

citations.report_title: Treystu þjóðinni — Opið bréf til forsætisráðherra Íslands

claims.claim_text: Ríkisstjórnin hefur upplýst að Alþingi muni boða til þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslu um framhald aðildarviðræðna við ESB þann 29. ágúst 2026.
